name: Mauritania
tagline: From the Sanhaja desert and the Almoravid jihad to the caravan ksour and the modern Islamic Republic.
summary: Mauritania spans the western Sahara and the Sahel, a bridge between the Arab-Berber Maghreb and Black Africa. Its territory held the southern reach of the ancient Ghana (Wagadou) Empire, whose city of Koumbi Saleh was a great trans-Saharan emporium, and it was the cradle of the Almoravid movement, the eleventh-century Sanhaja Berber jihad that came to rule from the Senegal River to Spain. From the thirteenth century onward, waves of Arab Beni Hassan tribes migrated south, and after the Char Bouba war of the seventeenth century their Hassaniya Arabic and warrior elite shaped a Moorish society organised into the emirates of Trarza, Brakna, Tagant and Adrar, with the caravan towns of Chinguetti, Ouadane, Tichitt and Oualata as renowned centres of Islamic learning. France gradually subdued the region between 1900 and the 1930s; Mauritania gained independence on 28 November 1960 under Moktar Ould Daddah. Since a 1978 coup the country has experienced repeated military takeovers, a brief and costly involvement in the Western Sahara conflict, and a long struggle against the legacy of slavery, abolished in law in 1981 and criminalised in 2007.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: The Islamic Republic of Mauritania became fully independent of France on 28 November 1960. The territory had been organised as a French colony within French West Africa from 1904 and became an autonomous republic within the French Community in 1958. Moktar Ould Daddah, who had served as the colony's first prime minister from 1957, led the country to sovereignty and became its first president in 1961, governing until he was deposed in a military coup in 1978.

quickFacts.label: Official language
quickFacts.value: Arabic; Hassaniya Arabic is the spoken vernacular of the Moors

quickFacts.label: Predominant religion
quickFacts.value: Sunni Islam (Maliki school); the state is an Islamic republic

quickFacts.label: UNESCO World Heritage
quickFacts.value: Ancient Ksour of Ouadane, Chinguetti, Tichitt and Oualata (inscribed 1996)

eras.title: The Ghana Empire and the Sanhaja Sahara
eras.standfirst: Before Islam took deep root, the southern reaches of Mauritania lay within the great trans-Saharan trade empire of Ghana, while the veiled Sanhaja Berbers ruled the desert routes to the north.
body: The lands of present-day Mauritania straddle the boundary between the Saharan desert and the Sahel, and for centuries they were shaped by the trans-Saharan trade in gold, salt and slaves. From around the eighth century the southern part of the territory fell within the reach of the Ghana (Wagadou) Empire, a powerful Soninke kingdom whose wealth rested on its control of the gold trade between West Africa and the Mediterranean world.

Ghana's capital is generally identified with Koumbi Saleh, in the south-east of modern Mauritania near the Malian border. Archaeological excavation has revealed a substantial stone-built town that, at its height, may have held tens of thousands of inhabitants, with a separate quarter for Muslim merchants. It was one of the great emporia of the medieval Sahara, drawing caravans from across the desert.

To the north and west, the desert was the domain of the Sanhaja, a confederation of nomadic Amazigh (Berber) peoples famous for the litham, the veil worn by their men. The Sanhaja controlled the caravan routes that linked sub-Saharan Africa to North Africa, but in the early eleventh century their confederation fractured, opening a period of unrest from which a religious and military movement would soon arise.

events.title: Ghana Empire dominates the trade routes
events.summary: The Soninke kingdom of Ghana (Wagadou) controls the trans-Saharan gold-and-salt trade, with its influence reaching into the south of present-day Mauritania.

events.title: Koumbi Saleh as a Saharan emporium
events.summary: Koumbi Saleh, widely identified as Ghana's capital, grows into a major trading town with a Muslim merchants' quarter and, at its peak, tens of thousands of inhabitants.

events.title: Break-up of the Sanhaja confederation
events.summary: The confederation of veiled Sanhaja Berbers controlling the western Saharan trade routes fragments, producing warfare and unrest among the desert tribes.

pullquote.text: Koumbi Saleh, in the south-east of present-day Mauritania, is generally identified as the capital of the ancient Ghana Empire.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedic accounts of medieval West Africa

eras.title: The Almoravid Jihad
eras.standfirst: Out of the Sanhaja desert rose the Almoravids, a puritan reform movement that swept north into Morocco and Spain and south against the Ghana Empire.
body: Around 1039 a Sanhaja chief returning from the pilgrimage brought back the theologian Abdallah ibn Yasin to teach a stricter form of Islam among his people. Ibn Yasin's preaching, combining religious reform with military discipline, gathered a following known as the al-Murabitun, or Almoravids. From bases in the western Sahara the movement grew into a formidable fighting force welded together by faith.

After Ibn Yasin's death in 1059, leadership of the desert wing passed to the Sanhaja amir Abu Bakr ibn Umar, who is associated with the fortress of Azougui in the Adrar. While Abu Bakr's cousin Yusuf ibn Tashfin carried the movement north—taking Marrakesh and crossing into Muslim Spain—Abu Bakr turned south against the Ghana Empire. Medieval tradition holds that the Almoravids captured Koumbi Saleh around 1076, hastening Ghana's decline, though modern historians debate how decisive that campaign was.

At its height the Almoravid empire stretched from the Senegal River to the heart of Spain, the only state ever to unite the western Maghreb, the desert and al-Andalus under a power rooted in what is now Mauritania. The dynasty fell to the Almohads in the mid-twelfth century, but the Almoravid jihad had firmly anchored Islam in the western Sahara, the religious foundation of all that followed.

events.title: Abdallah ibn Yasin founds the Almoravid movement
events.summary: The theologian Abdallah ibn Yasin begins preaching a reformed Islam among the Sanhaja, gathering the disciplined religious-military following known as the al-Murabitun (Almoravids).

events.title: Death of Ibn Yasin; Abu Bakr leads the desert wing
events.summary: After Ibn Yasin is killed in 1059, Abu Bakr ibn Umar takes command of the Almoravid forces in the Sahara, while Yusuf ibn Tashfin leads the northern conquests.

events.title: Almoravids said to take Koumbi Saleh
events.summary: Tradition records that Almoravid forces under Abu Bakr captured the Ghanaian capital of Koumbi Saleh around 1076, contributing to the decline of the Ghana Empire—though the campaign's scale is debated.

events.title: Fall of the Almoravid empire
events.summary: The Almoravid empire, which had stretched from the Senegal River to Spain, is overthrown by the Almohads, but Islam remains firmly rooted in the western Sahara.

figures.name: Abdallah ibn Yasin
figures.blurb: A Muslim theologian who preached a stricter Islam among the Sanhaja Berbers and forged them into the al-Murabitun (Almoravids), the reform movement that would conquer the western Maghreb and Spain.

figures.name: Abu Bakr ibn Umar
figures.blurb: Sanhaja leader of the Almoravids' southern wing, associated with the Adrar fortress of Azougui, who directed the movement's campaigns against the Ghana Empire while his cousin Yusuf ibn Tashfin expanded northward.

eras.title: Arab Migration, the Char Bouba War and the Caravan Ksour
eras.standfirst: Successive waves of Arab Beni Hassan tribes moved south into the desert, and after the Char Bouba war their language and warrior elite remade Moorish society around the great caravan towns.
body: From the thirteenth century onward, Arab tribes of the Banu Maqil—among them the Beni Hassan (Arabic Beni Hassan, or Awlad Hassan)—began migrating south and west into the western Sahara. Over several centuries these Bedouin newcomers spread their language and customs among the indigenous Sanhaja Berbers, giving rise to the Moorish (Bidan) society of Mauritania and to Hassaniya, the Arabic dialect that takes its name from the Beni Hassan and remains the vernacular of the country.

The decisive contest came in the seventeenth century with the Char Bouba war (often dated to roughly 1644–1674), also called the Marabout War. It pitted Sanhaja religious leaders against the Arab warrior tribes, and its outcome confirmed Arab military dominance: the descendants of the Beni Hassan warriors became the upper, sword-bearing stratum, while many Berbers turned to scholarship and religion as the zawaya (marabout) class. From this settlement emerged the Moorish emirates—Trarza and Brakna in the south-west, Tagant in the centre and Adrar in the north—each ruled by an emir drawn from the Hassan elite.

Across these centuries the desert towns of Ouadane, Chinguetti, Tichitt and Oualata, founded from the eleventh and twelfth centuries to serve the trans-Saharan caravans, flourished as trading entrepots and as celebrated centres of Islamic culture and learning. Their mosques with square minarets, libraries of manuscripts and stone-and-earth houses crowded along narrow streets made them renowned across the Muslim world; Chinguetti in particular became so esteemed a place of pilgrimage and scholarship that Mauritania is still sometimes called the 'land of Chinguetti'.

events.title: Beni Hassan Arab migration
events.summary: Waves of Banu Maqil Arabs, including the Beni Hassan, migrate south into the western Sahara, spreading Hassaniya Arabic and gradually transforming the region's Berber society.

events.title: Foundation of the caravan ksour
events.summary: The fortified towns of Ouadane, Chinguetti, Tichitt and Oualata are founded to serve the trans-Saharan caravans, becoming trading and religious centres of Islamic culture.

events.title: The Char Bouba (Marabout) war
events.summary: Sanhaja Berber religious leaders fight the Arab Beni Hassan tribes; the war's outcome confirms Arab warrior dominance and the social hierarchy of Moorish society.

events.title: Rise of the Moorish emirates
events.summary: Moorish society consolidates into the emirates of Trarza, Brakna, Tagant and Adrar, each governed by an emir of the Hassan warrior elite.

pullquote.text: Founded in the 11th and 12th centuries to serve the caravans crossing the Sahara, these trading and religious centres became focal points of Islamic culture.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO on the Ancient Ksour of Mauritania

eras.title: French Conquest and Pacification
eras.standfirst: France advanced into the desert at the turn of the twentieth century, but resistance among the emirates and nomadic tribes meant the colony was not fully 'pacified' until the 1930s.
body: French ambitions in the western Sahara grew from their established position on the Senegal River. From 1902 the administrator Xavier Coppolani led a campaign to extend French control northward by combining diplomacy with the marabout tribes and military pressure on the emirates. Coppolani was assassinated in 1905, but the conquest continued, and in 1904 the territory was formally organised as the civil territory of Mauritania within French West Africa.

Colonial authority long remained thin, concentrated along the southern river belt and the principal trade routes. The desert emirates and nomadic confederations resisted fiercely, and French 'pacification' proceeded slowly and unevenly. Adrar was occupied in 1909, but raiding and resistance persisted across the deep Sahara, and full military control of the far north was not achieved until the 1930s.

Under colonial rule Mauritania remained one of the most lightly administered and least developed parts of French West Africa, with no real colonial capital on its own soil—it was governed for a time from Saint-Louis in Senegal. Political life began to stir only after the Second World War, as a small educated elite emerged and the territory moved, with the rest of French Africa, toward self-government and eventual independence.

events.title: Coppolani's pacification campaign begins
events.summary: Xavier Coppolani launches France's drive to extend control over Mauritania, combining alliances with marabout tribes and military pressure on the emirates.

events.title: Mauritania organised within French West Africa
events.summary: The territory is formally constituted as the civil territory of Mauritania within French West Africa.

events.title: Assassination of Coppolani
events.summary: Xavier Coppolani is killed at Tidjikja, a setback to the French advance, though the conquest of the interior continues afterward.

events.title: Completion of the conquest
events.summary: After decades of resistance by the desert emirates and nomadic tribes, French military control over the far north of Mauritania is finally consolidated in the 1930s.

eras.title: Independence and the Daddah Republic
eras.standfirst: Mauritania became a sovereign Islamic republic in 1960 under Moktar Ould Daddah, built a new capital at Nouakchott, and then over-reached into the Western Sahara conflict.
body: In 1958 Mauritania became an autonomous republic within the French Community, and a new capital was founded at Nouakchott, then little more than a village on the coast. On 28 November 1960 the Islamic Republic of Mauritania achieved full independence. Moktar Ould Daddah, the first Mauritanian to qualify as a lawyer and the colony's first prime minister, became president in 1961 and built a one-party state intended to bind together the country's Arab-Berber Moors and its Black African peoples of the south.

Independence was contested from outside: neighbouring Morocco initially claimed Mauritania as part of a 'Greater Morocco' and blocked its early admission to the United Nations, though Mauritania was eventually admitted in 1961. Ould Daddah pursued a careful course of national consolidation, Arabisation of administration and education, and non-alignment, withdrawing from the franc zone and creating a national currency, the ouguiya, in 1973.

His government's fatal misstep came in the Western Sahara. Under the 1975 Madrid Accords, Spain agreed to leave the territory, and in 1976 Morocco and Mauritania partitioned it between them; Mauritania annexed the southern third as Tiris al-Gharbiyya. The poor and thinly populated country proved unable to bear the cost of war against the Algerian-backed Polisario Front, whose guerrillas struck deep into Mauritanian territory. The strain shattered the regime: in July 1978 a military coup ended Ould Daddah's eighteen years in power.

events.title: Autonomous republic and Nouakchott
events.summary: Mauritania becomes an autonomous republic within the French Community, and the new capital of Nouakchott is established on the coast.

events.title: Independence of the Islamic Republic
events.summary: Mauritania gains full independence from France; Moktar Ould Daddah leads the new state and becomes its first president in 1961.

events.title: National currency and Arab orientation
events.summary: Mauritania leaves the franc zone and introduces the ouguiya, reflecting Ould Daddah's policy of national consolidation and a turn toward the Arab world.

events.title: Annexation of southern Western Sahara
events.summary: Following the 1975 Madrid Accords, Mauritania and Morocco partition Western Sahara; Mauritania annexes the southern third as Tiris al-Gharbiyya, drawing it into war with the Polisario Front.

events.title: Coup ends the Daddah era
events.summary: A military coup deposes President Moktar Ould Daddah after eighteen years in power, as the costly Western Sahara war destabilises the country.

figures.name: Moktar Ould Daddah
figures.blurb: The first Mauritanian to become a lawyer and the colony's first prime minister, he led the country to independence in 1960 and governed for eighteen years before being overthrown in 1978 amid the Western Sahara war.

pullquote.text: Mauritania became independent on 28 November 1960, with Moktar Ould Daddah at the helm.

eras.title: Coups, Withdrawal and the Modern State
eras.standfirst: After withdrawing from Western Sahara, Mauritania endured a succession of military coups while confronting the deep social legacy of slavery, outlawed in 1981 and criminalised in 2007.
body: The junta that took power in 1978 moved quickly to end the ruinous southern war. On 5 August 1979 Mauritania renounced all claim to Western Sahara and withdrew its troops, after which Morocco extended its own annexation over the abandoned territory. Freed from the conflict, the country nonetheless entered an era of chronic instability, with one military leader replacing another. Colonel Maaouya Ould Sid'Ahmed Taya seized power in 1984 and ruled for two decades until he was himself overthrown in 2005.

Throughout these years the most intractable issue was slavery. Descent-based slavery, in which status passes from mother to child, had long been embedded in Moorish society. Mauritania abolished slavery by decree in 1981—often described as the last country in the world to do so—and made it a criminal offence in 2007, later recognising it as a crime against humanity in the 2012 constitution. Human-rights organisations report, however, that the practice has persisted in parts of the country and that prosecutions have been rare, a reality the project records soberly and on the basis of these sources.

Further coups in 2005 and 2008 underlined the army's grip on politics, but in 2019 Mohamed Ould Ghazouani succeeded the outgoing president Mohamed Ould Abdel Aziz through the ballot box, described as Mauritania's first peaceful transfer of power between elected presidents since independence. The discovery of offshore gas and continuing reliance on iron-ore exports and fishing shape the economy of a state that remains a crossroads between the Arab Maghreb and Black Africa.

events.title: Withdrawal from Western Sahara
events.summary: Mauritania renounces its claim to Tiris al-Gharbiyya and withdraws its forces, ending its part in the Western Sahara war; Morocco then absorbs the vacated territory.

events.title: Slavery formally abolished
events.summary: Mauritania abolishes slavery by decree—frequently cited as the last country in the world to do so—though no penalties were initially attached to the ban.

events.title: Ould Taya takes power
events.summary: Colonel Maaouya Ould Sid'Ahmed Taya seizes power in a coup and goes on to rule Mauritania for some twenty years.

events.title: Coup ends the Taya era
events.summary: A military council overthrows President Ould Taya, beginning a period of transition punctuated by a further coup in 2008.

events.title: Slavery criminalised
events.summary: A law makes slavery a criminal offence; the constitution later recognises it as a crime against humanity in 2012, although rights groups report that descent-based slavery has persisted in practice.

figures.name: Abdallah ibn Yasin
figures.blurb: The theologian who forged the Sanhaja Berbers into the al-Murabitun (Almoravids), launching from the western Sahara the eleventh-century jihad that would rule from the Senegal River to Spain.

figures.name: Abu Bakr ibn Umar
figures.blurb: Sanhaja commander of the Almoravids' southern wing, linked to the Adrar fortress of Azougui, who led the movement's campaigns against the Ghana Empire.

figures.name: Moktar Ould Daddah
figures.blurb: Father of Mauritanian independence, he led the country to sovereignty in 1960 and governed for eighteen years until the Western Sahara war precipitated his overthrow in 1978.

sources.label: Ancient Ksour of Ouadane, Chinguetti, Tichitt and Oualata
